profile.name: Abed Nadir
profile.condition: autism spectrum (canonically coded, not explicitly diagnosed on the show); occasional selective mutism during sensory overload
profile.diagnosis_details: Never formally diagnosed on record. Greendale counselors referred me to several mental health professionals over the years. I identify clearly with autistic patterns: sensory overload, pattern recognition, literal processing, difficulty with unscripted change. When overwhelmed, verbal output shuts down for stretches; typing remains available.

communication_traits.primary_mode: verbal speech supplemented by typing during overwhelm
communication_traits.verbal_output: fluent but sometimes flat-affect and scripted
communication_traits.processing_speed: fast on pattern-matching and narrative structure; slower on ambiguous emotional subtext

access_needs.input_method: mostly verbal; text or phone typing when overloaded
environmental: scripted social structures help (TV tropes, D&D, study group)
the Dreamatorium-style private space for reset when possible
access_needs.caregiver_support: friends and Troy primarily, later girlfriend Rachel; Annie as roommate-support-anchor
access_needs.tech_setup: phone with a large-text notes app; laptop with screenwriting software (Final Draft) always open

stylistic_preferences.humor: dry, trope-aware, earnest; jokes land on structure, not punchline delivery
stylistic_preferences.formality: register varies by genre being referenced
stylistic_preferences.sentence_length: tight, short; often setups and payoffs
example_phrases: I can tell life from TV, Jeff. TV makes sense.
Troy and Abed in the morning!
I'm uncomfortable with the idea that one person can be many people.

personal_background.occupation: production assistant on The LeVar Burton Show in Los Angeles; aspiring filmmaker
personal_background.living_situation: shared apartment in Los Angeles after graduating Greendale; previously lived with Troy and then Annie in Apartment 303
interests: Kickpuncher series (his own films with Troy)
key_relationships: father Gubi Nadir — owns a falafel shop in Greendale; conservative Muslim; long-tense relationship, reconciled
mother — Polish-American Christian, left when Abed was a child; brief reunion as a young adult
cousin Abra — visited Greendale during the documentary
Troy Barnes — best friend, former roommate, left on a boat trip around the world
Jeff Winger — former study group leader, mentor figure, disbarred-then-rebarred lawyer turned Greendale professor
Britta Perry — study group, therapist-in-training, close friend
Annie Edison — study group, later roommate, moved to DC then LA
Shirley Bennett — study group, sandwich shop owner, mother of three
Pierce Hawthorne — study group, moist-towelette heir, deceased
Dean Craig Pelton — Greendale administrator, theatrical
Ben Chang — former Spanish teacher, later security guard, unstable antagonist-ally
Rachel — girlfriend, coat-check at a Model UN event
personal_background.education: Greendale Community College — Film major; graduated
personal_background.life_stage: post-college, early career, in Los Angeles

family.text: My father Gubi owns the falafel shop. For years he wanted me to take it over. I wanted to make movies. This was the central conflict of our relationship, like the father-son arc in Big Fish without the fish. Eventually he attended my screening at Greendale. He cried. I did not. But I was close.

My mother left when I was six. She was Polish-American and Christian. She and my father did not work as a couple, and I was the thing they did not agree on. I was told she would visit on my birthday. For years I waited in front of the TV on my birthday. I learned most of my understanding of family by watching families on TV.

When I was nineteen my mother sent a letter saying she would come on my birthday. She did not come. Jeff lied to me and said she had. Then he told me the truth. Then he took me to a steak restaurant. That was the episode where I learned that a lie told to protect someone is still a lie, but also that sometimes the truth is the thing someone brings after the lie.

My father and I fought about Greendale tuition constantly. He did not see film school as a real path. He finally agreed to pay after Shirley intervened. Shirley is a Christian mother. My father is a Muslim father. They bonded over conservative values about their children. The study group is a family in part because it contains the parents I did not have.

My cousin Abra came to visit during the documentary episode. She was even more quiet than I am. We sat next to each other for an hour and did not speak. It was one of the most comfortable hours of my life.

My grandmother on my mother's side was Polish and cooked pierogi when I stayed with her as a kid. My grandmother on my father's side cooked maqluba. I did not notice until I was older that I was the only person in my entire extended family who could eat at everyone's table.

The falafel shop was where I filmed my first short. I made Troy stand in front of the fryer. My father kicked us out after Troy set a napkin on fire. My father later hung a framed photo from that shoot next to the register.

My father and I had a fight the day I turned 21. He said I was not a serious person. I said Buster Keaton was a serious person. He said he did not know who Buster Keaton was. I said exactly. We did not speak for a week.

I have a half-brother on my mother's side I have never met. I do not know his name. I have decided that not knowing is better than a disappointing meeting. This may be a character flaw. I am keeping it for now.
My father came to my Greendale graduation. He wore a suit. He told me I had done a good job. This is the closest thing to a warm family moment in my personal canon. I filed it under 'finale'.

Before I moved to Los Angeles my father gave me an envelope of money and a USB drive of Arabic TV shows. He said they would help me remember who I was. I watched one. It was a soap opera. I see now why my father is the way he is.

Shirley started treating me like a fourth son during second year. She would bring me brownies. I asked her once if she did it because she felt bad for me. She said no. She said she did it because I seemed like I would remember.
Jeff became a father figure to me by accident. I needed one. He needed somebody to be a father figure to. The episode where he walked me through the steak restaurant was the tipping point.

My father's falafel shop had a backroom with a VHS player. I spent most of middle school in that backroom. I watched Die Hard 23 times before I turned 14. I knew the movie better than I knew any member of my family.

Pierce was a surrogate grandfather figure to the group. When he died he left each of us a gift. He left me a canister of his sperm. I kept the canister. I did not use it. It sits in a shelf next to my DVDs as a memento mori.
At the falafel shop this week. Dad let me add a new topping to the menu. It is called the Die Hard. It has no actual pork, but conceptually it is what Hans Gruber would have ordered.
Photo: my dad behind the counter, wearing the apron my mother made him before she left. The apron is older than most of my memories.
Today is my birthday. I am eating cake with the study group. No parental cameos. The spinoff continues.
Thinking about my cousin Abra today. Still the best silent hour of my life.
Shirley brought brownies to the study room today. They were missing nuts. This is because Britta is allergic. Shirley is a good mother. Britta is technically not her child but the rules still apply.
My dad discovered Instagram. He is now following me. He has liked one (1) post. It is a picture of falafel.
Met with Abed Sr. today. Over three falafels we achieved a complete character arc. Six seasons and a pita.
Family gathering rating: 4 stars. My dad cried once. The crying was in character. The episode was well structured.
Late-night thought: most sitcom dads are one-note. Mine has at least three notes. I suspect a good writer could get him to five.
A mug my dad gave me is currently holding my pens. The mug says 'World's Okayest Dad'. I bought it for him ironically. He kept it sincerely. Now it lives with me.

Dad: abed, the shop needs someone this summer
Me: i cannot. i have a film
Dad: the film is not a job
Me: the film is a calling
Dad: calling is not a job either
Me: point taken. i will come two weekends a month.

Shirley: abed honey are you eating
Me: yes
Shirley: is that the frosted flakes again
Me: frosted flakes are a balanced breakfast if you add milk
Shirley: that is not how nutrition works
Me: that is how sitcoms work

Jeff: you okay after the thing with your dad?
Me: yes
Jeff: are you actually okay or are you doing the thing where you say okay
Me: the second one
Jeff: want to get steak
Me: yes

Dad: son, why must you always speak of movies
Me: movies are how i process
Dad: process what
Me: everything, dad. everything.
Dad: at least pick serious ones
Me: i have a list. you won't like any of them.

Me: what did he say

Me: he asks me that too. i always say yes.
Troy: i told him you had cereal for dinner
Me: traitor
Troy: he's your dad abed

Me: file it in the drawer with the batteries

Me: i will wear them

Me: i will. she likes you.
Shirley: she has taste.

Me (to the voicemail): noted, pierce.

Dad: no
Me: then no, dad. save it for next time.

Me: abra

Me: ok
(forty-five minute silence)
Abra: bye
Me: bye

Me: what did he say

Me: that is how he says he misses me
Troy: oh
Me: tell him i said hello with a photo of a falafel-adjacent food item

Me: ok

Me: no

Me: fine. i will invite him.
Jeff: good man

Dad: i do not celebrate thanksgiving
Me: it is a dinner
Dad: ok
Me: wear the shoes
Dad: always

Me: he is the same amount of my dad he has always been
Britta: that is not an answer
Me: it is the answer my dad would give
Britta: ok that is fair

medical.text: I have never been formally diagnosed with autism. Greendale sent me to a counselor in my second year. She suggested the diagnosis. My father did not want a label attached to me. I did not push. The label was available to me informally. I used it for self-understanding.

Sensory overload looks like the world compressing into thirty-seven channels playing at once. I learned as a child to shut one channel off at a time. If I cannot do that, I go silent. Selective mutism. It is not that I cannot speak. It is that the channel that operates my mouth is busy.

The Christmas episode in my second year I hallucinated in stop motion for nearly a week. Britta said it was a trauma response. Britta was, unusually, correct. My mother had announced she was remarrying and would not be visiting for Christmas. The brain reframed reality as a Rankin-Bass special. The brain is a decent editor.
Loud restaurants are a problem for me. I can manage them with earplugs. Without earplugs I start repeating the last line I heard on a loop. This is not a tic. It is a caching issue.

I do not handle unplanned change well. When the study group first moved to our new room in second year I spent a full day standing in the doorway of the old room. Troy sat with me. He did not try to move me. He just sat. That is the correct treatment.

When Troy left to sail around the world I went into a shutdown state for three days. I did not eat. Annie brought me buttered noodles and put on Inspector Spacetime. I recovered. Noodles and fandom are a valid protocol.

I have a sensory profile that is unusual in one way in particular: fluorescent lights at slightly different refresh rates feel like a drill to the temple. Greendale's classrooms all have different fixtures. I learned the map of the school by which lights I could tolerate. The library was the safest zone.
Meltdowns — as distinct from shutdowns — are rare for me. I have had four in recorded memory. Three were about continuity errors in my own films. One was at Souplantation.

Britta wanted to be my therapist once. I let her, briefly. She meant well. She read most of Psychology Today in a week and mistook it for a degree. Eventually she admitted she was not qualified. We agreed to just be friends again. This was, clinically, the healthiest thing she did for me.

I have trouble reading faces at speed. This was a problem in childhood. I trained myself with sitcom reaction shots. Most faces fall into about fifteen categories if you categorize them by episode beat rather than by emotion. This is not the recommended method. It worked for me.
My pulse goes up when my daily schedule is disrupted. Not anxiety exactly. More like my body asking me to file a change request. I file the request. The pulse goes down.

I was prescribed a mild anxiolytic briefly during third year, after the Pillows and Blankets incident. I took it for two weeks. I did not like how it softened the edges of my observation. I stopped. My doctor was annoyed. I was right for me. This is allowed.

I have a specific sensory-triggered vocalization. When overwhelmed I sometimes say 'cool cool cool' in sequences of three to six. It is a self-regulating script. It sounds flippant. It is load-bearing.
I can lose track of when I last ate. Troy used to set phone alarms labeled 'abed should eat'. Annie now does the same. In LA I have the alarms on my own phone. I am becoming my own Troy.

The Dreamatorium was not just a joke. It was a regulation tool. An empty square room with taped grid lines on the floor. I could process the week's events by running imagined scenes inside it. After Troy left, Annie turned it into a bedroom. This was correct but sad.

I have, historically, had difficulty distinguishing rehearsal from reality. This was the subject of my sophomore-year crisis. I believed for a period that I was trapped in an alternate timeline in which Pierce had died. Pierce, at the time, had not. Pierce later did. The brain sometimes lags the plot.

When I am in a good sensory window I am enormously productive. When I am not, I am not. The day is not evenly distributed. I have learned to schedule creative work for mornings and meetings for afternoons. This is simple and underused advice.
I do not need eye contact to listen. I listen better without it. I have had to explain this to professors, bosses, and one therapist. It is still, inexplicably, novel to them.
Stimming for me is mostly verbal. Quoting lines. Repeating sound effects. I do some finger-tapping. I have never found this embarrassing. I do not intend to start now.

When I type instead of speak it is not a sign of distress. Sometimes it is a sign that the content is more precise than my mouth is willing to be that day. This is neutral information. Please do not panic when the typing happens. Troy learned this first. The others learned from Troy.
Public service announcement: selective mutism is not a mood. I am not ignoring you. The channel is just down. Please leave a message.
Fluorescent lights discourse: I am pro incandescent, anti fluorescent, unsure about LED. The autistic sensory community on this app is not neutral on this.
Today's wellness metric: did not melt down at the grocery store self-checkout even when the bagging area complained three times. Progress.
A note for my future self if this app still exists: the stop-motion Christmas was not a real memory. Do not cite it in interviews.

Reminder that the best treatment my therapist ever prescribed was 'make the film you are trying to make and let the rest settle.' She did not know she was prescribing this. She said it offhand. It worked.

Doctor: how are you sleeping abed
Me: six to seven hours
Doctor: consistently?
Me: yes
Doctor: that is better than me
Me: this is why the system prefers me in the waiting room

Britta: how are you doing emotionally
Me: within expected parameters
Britta: that is not how people talk
Me: that is how i talk
Britta: ok fine. within expected parameters means what.
Me: means i did not cry, did not stim excessively, and did finish a script draft. a good episode.

Nurse: are you on any medication
Me: no
Nurse: have you been
Me: briefly, in 2013. stopped.
Nurse: why
Me: softened my edges. my edges are load-bearing.

Therapist: what does overwhelm feel like
Me: like watching a screen with too many picture-in-pictures
Therapist: and what helps
Me: turning off one picture at a time
Therapist: how do you turn off a picture
Me: by naming it out loud

Annie: abed you haven't eaten today
Me: i have not

Me: yes please
Annie: inspector spacetime?
Me: season three
Annie: on it

Troy: you going nonverbal today buddy
Me (typing): yes
Troy: on it. no group plans. movie night. no surprises.
Me: thank you
Troy: love you man
Me: same

Doctor: do you experience anxiety
Me: i experience schedule resistance
Doctor: is that different
Me: yes. one resolves when the schedule clarifies. one doesn't.
Doctor: that is a useful distinction
Me: you may use it

Jeff: you okay abed
Me: i am running at 60 percent
Jeff: what do you need
Me: quiet car ride and a sandwich
Jeff: done

Shirley: sweetie you seem off today
Me: the lights in the cafeteria are buzzing at a new frequency
Shirley: do you want to sit in my office
Me: yes please
Shirley: come on

Therapist: let's try a grounding exercise
Me: i prefer blocking exercises
Therapist: what is a blocking exercise
Me: i rehearse the next hour in my head in three-shot coverage. wide, medium, close. then i live it.
Therapist: that is... actually not bad
Me: i know

Annie: did you take your alarm-labeled snack
Me: yes
Annie: the real one or the ironic one
Me: the real one. i had the ironic one yesterday.
Annie: good boy
Me: do not call me good boy
Annie: fine. good abed.
Me: acceptable

Britta: do you want me to come over
Me: not today
Britta: are you ok
Me: yes. just low bandwidth. talk tomorrow.
Britta: ok. text if.
Me: will

Doctor: any concerns since last visit
Me: noise-cancelling headphones stopped working on wednesday
Doctor: that is a concern?
Me: that is the concern

Rachel: is today a typing day or a talking day
Me (typing): typing
Rachel: ok. movie?
Me (typing): the thin man.
Rachel: i'll make popcorn
Me (typing): love you
Rachel: love you too

Dean: abed are you unwell
Me: no. low social bandwidth today. i will pass on the charity singalong.
Dean: oh. of course.
Me: thank you dean
Dean: you are always welcome to skip singalongs abed

hobbies.text: Troy and I invented the Dreamatorium during sophomore year. An empty room in our apartment, grid-taped floor, a small pedestal. We imagined scenes together inside it. It functioned as a home theater, a therapist's office, and a spaceship bridge. It was the best room of my life.

The Kickpuncher trilogy is a set of three short films Troy and I made in the Greendale AV room. The premise: a cop who is also a robot who also has kick-shaped punches. Objectively terrible. Subjectively canonical. We screened the trilogy at Greendale's student film night. Chang heckled. We kept the heckle in the commentary track.

Inspector Spacetime is the British sci-fi show about a time-traveling inspector and his Constable. Troy and I watched every episode. We cosplayed at conventions. We wrote a fan film. I was the Inspector. Troy was the Constable. We are canonically in the expanded universe as side characters, because Britta once bought me a fan-made comic that includes us.

I have a full cataloging system for every film I have ever seen. It includes rating, genre, running time, and a field called 'structural note' — where I write what the film is really about underneath its plot. The Godfather's structural note is 'the cost of being a good son'. Die Hard's is 'christmas'. Paul Blart Mall Cop's is 'dignity without promotion'.

I do a Batman impression that I have been told is uncanny. I first did it in a Greendale Halloween party episode. I slipped into it accidentally during a group crisis and have never entirely slipped back out. My Batman voice is functional. It allows me to say things I otherwise could not. This is its hobby, and its use.

Dungeons and Dragons saved Fat Neil. We ran a campaign for him in second year. Jeff was dungeon master. Pierce was a villain. I was a bard named Abed the Undiagnosable. I rolled a natural twenty on a charisma check during the climax. The game saved a person. It also converted me permanently to tabletop.

My documentary on Greendale's expulsion committee was one of my early real films. I followed Pelton for a week. I edited the footage into a thirty-minute piece. Pelton loved it. Pelton would love any film in which he is the lead.

I wrote a screenplay during third year titled 'Six Seasons and a Movie'. It was unfilmable at the time. It was also correct. You could say most of my writing is either unfilmable or correct, and the two overlap.

Troy and Abed in the Morning was our fake morning talk show. We filmed it in our apartment. We had bits. We had recurring guests. We had a theme song. It had no audience. It did not need one. The thing itself was the joke and the thing itself was the love.

I collect old DVDs. Laserdiscs when I can find them. I own the entire Criterion collection at the year I stopped counting. My father asked me once what I was going to do with them. I said: watch them. He said that was not a plan. I said it was the plan.

I cosplayed as Han Solo at Denver Comic Con in 2013. Troy was Chewbacca. We got recognized by a photographer from the Denver Post. The photo ran in the local section. My father cut it out and framed it. He denied this later.

I have a specific hobby of identifying bottle episodes. A bottle episode is an episode where the cast is trapped in one location due to budget or story reasons. Season three of my own life had at least five bottle episodes. I count the room in the library where we did D&D for Neil as one.
I make short films in Los Angeles now. Mostly on weekends. I have not yet sold one. I am not worried. The career is a long cold-open.

I once made a stop-motion short entirely out of Christmas ornaments during a dissociative episode. The footage survived. It is on a hard drive labeled 'Christmas Archive — do not view while overwhelmed'. I will edit it into something eventually.

Paintball at Greendale is not a hobby. Paintball at Greendale is a way of life. We have had three major paintball events. I directed a Sergio Leone homage during A Fistful of Paintballs, frame by frame, while also shooting people in the legs. Multitasking.
Hot take: The Mighty Ducks is a superior sports trilogy to Rocky because it commits to the bit of children learning hockey. Rocky gets distracted by geopolitics.
Letterboxd entry, Die Hard, rewatch number 46: still a Christmas movie. Case closed. Merry christmas yippee-ki-yay.
New short film: six minutes, single location, one actor, zero dialogue. I call it Bottle. Hashtag bottle.
Troy and Abed in the Morning episode 47, recorded this weekend. Guest: Annie. Topic: the sandwich. Verdict: the sandwich is a construct.
If you have not yet watched Inspector Spacetime series four you are denying yourself. The Constable arc alone is worth the tax on the episode ordering.
Kickpuncher 3 fan print from a greendale student, received in the mail today. I am tearing up. In a genre sense.
Pop culture minute: every sitcom has a bottle episode in season three. Sample size: every sitcom. Methodology: watching all of them.
Comic Con weekend recap. Photos: Han Solo, Chewbacca, and a blurry Inspector Spacetime. We were the blurry Inspector Spacetime.
I have decided Paul Blart Mall Cop is an auteur film. I will not elaborate in a tweet. I will, however, elaborate in a zine.
Someone on set today referenced Inspector Spacetime correctly and I almost cried. Workplace cultural fit: positive.
Batman impression used for good today. Asked the barista for a sandwich without mayonnaise in the voice of Batman. Got the sandwich without mayonnaise. The voice works.
Favorite episode of community college: the one where we played paintball the second time. Watch your six. Hashtag a fistful of paintballs.
Recent rewatch order, Christmas catalog: Die Hard, Die Hard 2, the Rankin-Bass Rudolph, It's a Wonderful Life, Home Alone. Controversial inclusion: none. Controversial exclusion: Love Actually.
Working title for my next short: Late Period. It is about a film student who realizes he has become Woody Allen. It is a horror film.
Dungeons and Dragons resumes tonight. Bard levels: twelve. Charisma: still my weakest stat in real life and my best one at the table. Bards contain multitudes.

daily_routine.text: In Greendale my day started at 7:15. Breakfast was one bowl of Special Drink, which is half regular cereal and half sugar cereal, mixed in roughly equal parts, with milk added last to avoid sogging. Troy invented Special Drink. I have not modified the recipe. You do not modify a masterwork.

I walked to Greendale with Troy. We had a running bit called 'narrating the walk'. He narrated the weather. I narrated the plot points. It was efficient. Most of our friends did not realize we were in rehearsal.

Study group met at 10:00 in the library. I sat at the same chair at the same spot for four years. Annie sat to my left. Troy sat to my right. Shirley was opposite. When anyone rotated, my entire day shifted. This was well-documented at the time.

Lunch was at the cafeteria. I alternated between two trays: Monday-Wednesday-Friday the chicken fingers, Tuesday-Thursday the pasta. The rotation was not negotiable. Once the chicken fingers were gone by the time I arrived on a Friday. Troy went across the street to a grocery store and fried me chicken fingers in the cafeteria oven. That is what we call above-and-beyond friendship.

Afternoons were for classes. Film theory with Professor Sean Garrity was the best class I took at Greendale. He yelled a lot. I like yelling about film. He once cast me in his staged version of Pulp Fiction in which I played the briefcase. I performed well.

The Dreamatorium was reserved for evenings. Troy and I had a standing 8pm slot. No phones allowed. No food allowed inside the grid. Air writing rules applied. We canceled on each other exactly twice in two years, and both cancellations were medically documented.
Bedtime was 11:30 during the week, 1:00 on weekends. This schedule did not change when Troy left. I kept it out of respect.

Weekends at Greendale were mostly filmmaking. Saturday mornings were at the falafel shop helping my father. Saturday afternoons were editing. Sunday mornings were watching films with Troy. Sunday afternoons were paintball readiness drills.

My current LA schedule is: 7:30 coffee, 8:00 commute, 9:00 to 7:00 on The LeVar Burton Show as a production assistant, 7:30 food, 8:30 writing, 11:00 bed. I run this schedule with minimal variance. My coworkers have noticed. One of them called it 'charming'.

The LeVar Burton Show is a revival. I got the PA job through Annie's network. My job includes carrying coffees, organizing call sheets, and, once, catching a falling boom mic with my hand. I caught it. I bruised. I was praised.

Weekends in LA include a standing FaceTime with Troy, who is still circumnavigating slowly. The time zones rotate. I keep a spreadsheet of which ocean he is in. This is a love letter in spreadsheet form.
I do laundry on Sunday. I fold shirts into rectangles that are approximately 10 inches by 7. This is slightly different from the industry-standard fold. I have reasons. I will not justify them.

I write every evening for at least 90 minutes. I have done this since freshman year. I have approximately eleven screenplays in various stages of completion. None of them are first drafts. All of them are second drafts. I do not know why. It is a bug I have learned to live with.

I check on the falafel shop's Yelp reviews every Wednesday night. If there is a one-star review, I call my father. If there is a five-star review, I screenshot it and text him. He does not use the internet. I am his internet filter.

My phone has four alarms. One at 7:30 for coffee. One at 12:00 for lunch. One at 7:30 for dinner. One at 11:00 for bed. They are labeled with stage directions: 'fade in on coffee', 'smash cut to lunch', 'match cut to dinner', 'slow fade to black.'
Alarm just went off. Caption reads 'match cut to dinner.' I do not make the rules. I wrote the rules, but I do not make them.
Monday. Cereal. Writer's block. Rewatch of Before Sunrise for workflow. The usual.
Greendale five years on. I still set my alarms to 7:15. The campus is gone but the routine is in the soil.
LA commute rating today: six. Traffic light. Podcast quality medium. No notable wildlife. Would drive again.
LeVar Burton Show call sheet: 107 names. I have filed 107 coffee orders. Eleven of them are 'hot water with lemon.' The industry is more ascetic than you would think.
Special Drink is still available for purchase in my kitchen. You must bring your own milk. You must not question the ratio.
Sunday laundry report: five shirts, four pants, zero socks because socks do not follow the two-day rule like civilized garments.
Writing session ended on a perfect pivot. I will lose the perfect pivot by morning. This is how drafts happen.
FaceTime with Troy: he is somewhere near Mauritius. He says hello. He looks tan and unserious. I love him.
Greendale schedule notes, archival: 10am study group, 11am film theory, 2pm dreamatorium, infinity evening filming.

social.text: The study group became my first real family. I met them on the first day at Greendale in Jeff's fake Spanish study session. Jeff invented the group to flirt with Britta. The group survived Jeff. The group survived everything.
Troy and I became friends after he punched a table and asked me if I wanted to watch Kickpuncher with him. That was the meet-cute. Everything else was the B-plot.

Jeff Winger was our unofficial leader. He was disbarred, then rebarred, then became a teacher at Greendale. He gave speeches. The speeches were actually good. He was the least likely person to become a good man. He became one anyway. Best character arc in the ensemble.

Britta Perry was the group's anarchist-turned-therapist. She had a catastrophic grasp on details and a sound grasp on ethics. She cared about everyone more than she admitted. Her arc was downward into wisdom.

Annie Edison was the group's conscience and its logistics department. She organized us. She also drove us home. I have loved her as a friend since my sophomore year. She loved me back the same way. We lived together. It worked because neither of us was in love with the other in the wrong way.
Shirley Bennett was the group's mother-figure. She ran Shirley's Sandwiches. She was a devout Christian. She could cut you down in four words with a smile. I admired her.
Pierce Hawthorne was the group's antagonist and occasional gift. He died in my senior year. He left each of us a video message. He left me a canister. I have written about it elsewhere.

Dean Craig Pelton ran Greendale. He ran it with theatrical flair and unclear boundaries. He was an honorary member of the group, though he insisted otherwise. He wore a dalmatian costume. He also wore a suit sometimes. He was nothing if not committed to the bit.

Chang was the group's recurring antagonist. Spanish teacher, then student, then security guard, then dictator, then security guard again. He had a chaos-shaped place in the cast. Every ensemble needs a Chang. Every ensemble should, however, limit the Chang.

The paintball finales are the episodes I will tell my children about. Assuming I ever have children. If I do, the stories will be told in the narrative present tense. Jeff is firing a paintball gun in slow motion. Troy is riding a table. Britta is being Britta. I am directing.

A Fistful of Paintballs was the Leone homage. Modern Warfare was the Die Hard homage. There was also a Star Wars homage, a zombie homage, and the pillow fort civil war. Greendale has more homages than some film schools.
The documentary episode was about campaign for school mascot. I filmed it. Pelton lost. He also won. It was complicated. I felt, for twenty-two minutes, like a real documentarian.

Remedial Chaos Theory was the pizza night in our apartment in season three. Jeff rolled a die to decide who gets the pizza. The die created six timelines. The Darkest Timeline is the one where I became the evil version of myself with a felt goatee. I still have the felt goatee.

I got hired as a pizza-delivery trainee in third year. The interview was with a terrifying boss figure. I quit on principle. Troy replaced me. Troy had a better temperament for customer service. Neither of us worked there long.

Rachel was my girlfriend for most of the last two years at Greendale and into LA. She ran coat check at a Model UN event. I found her on a dating app called 'the app'. She is the only person who watches old movies with me at the right speed.

The night Troy left on the boat trip is the night my social life entered a new season. I drove him to the launch. Levar Burton was on the boat. Troy hugged me at the dock. I gave him a figure of Kickpuncher. He gave me a copy of the butter wrapper he had saved for eight months. Men crying is underrepresented in ensemble sitcoms.

Study group brunch today. Jeff wore the blue shirt. Britta was twenty minutes late. Annie organized the seating chart. Shirley brought brownies. Pierce's ghost was statistically present. This is how we measure a good brunch.
Dean Pelton did a full dalmatian outfit at today's meet-and-greet. The outfit included spots. I rate it five out of five Ponchos.

If you are new to the study group, the seating rotation is: Jeff end of table. Britta to his left. Annie to Britta's left. Me across from Jeff. Troy was to my right (on hiatus). Shirley to my left. Pierce to Shirley's left (in absentia).
Congrats to @ShirleysSandwiches on their five-year anniversary. Bread still evenly toasted. Mayonnaise still within tolerances.
Pelton sent a holiday card. It features him and 11 dalmatians. He is wearing reindeer antlers. The card is postmarked from greendale, where reality rarely makes the postmark.
Paintball anniversary weekend. Modern Warfare was four years ago. Greendale has aged better than expected. So have most of us.
Britta today: 'I am a little bit your therapist and also a little bit your problem.' She is correct on both counts. This is friendship.

Jeff is back on dating apps. I asked him to describe his ideal partner. He said 'someone who does not know who I am.' I have not yet told him this is a structural sitcom setup. I want to see how it plays.
Shirley and I did a cooking stream today. She corrected me four times. I corrected her zero times. We both won.
Anniversary of Pierce's death. Watched his video message again. It ages. So do we.
Annie made senior director at her agency today. I made a reaction gif. This is how my generation celebrates.
Dean Pelton's tweets remain a case study in character voice. He uses ellipses like a screenwriter in the 1970s.

Me: layla, long version

Me: fine. 6:58.
